message:
fix(stats): prevent usize overflow + bounded bar_len in render_stats_bar

render_stats_bar computed
    let bar_len = ((count as f64 / max as f64) * width as f64).round() as usize;
    let bar_plain = format!("{:width$}", "█".repeat(bar_len), width = width);

which is a two-step crash waiting to happen:

1. When max == 0, `count / 0` is +infinity (or NaN when count is
   also 0). `(inf).round() as usize` saturates to usize::MAX on
   current stable Rust, and `"█".repeat(usize::MAX)` then tries
   to allocate ~3 × usize::MAX bytes — the allocator aborts and
   kills the whole binary. The in-tree caller already `.max(1)`s
   max before invoking, so this was only latent, but nothing in
   the type system prevents a future caller from forgetting the
   invariant.

2. Even with a non-zero max, any unusual arithmetic shape (count >
   max from a stale snapshot, rounding quirks on oddly-sized
   widths) can produce bar_len > width. Rendering a bar wider
   than its alleged width is both visually wrong and allocates
   more than necessary.

Fix:

- Early return `" ".repeat(width)` when max == 0. Produces a
  correctly-sized empty bar for the degenerate input instead of
  crashing.
- After computing bar_len from the ratio, clamp with
  `bar_len.min(width)` so the repeat allocation is bounded by the
  declared width no matter what arithmetic went in. Doc-comment
  both guards so a future refactor that reintroduces the overflow
  (or drops the caller's .max(1) invariant) hits a clear "why
  this exists" note.

print_provider_stats has the same shape without the max==0 path
(its `total` is explicitly .max(1)'d at the binding site so
division is safe), but the bar_len result can still exceed width
via a stale-snapshot shape where count > total. Apply the same
`bar_len.min(width)` clamp there too for defense in depth, with
a comment pointing at the binding-site invariant and the failure
mode it defends against.

No behaviour change on the happy path. Both guards only fire in
cases that previously aborted the process or produced oversized
bars.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
